services.name: VPC
services.description: Provision a logically isolated section of AWS where you launch resources in a virtual network you define. Full control over IP ranges, subnets, route tables, and gateways.
keyPoints: CIDR blocks: define IP address range (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16 = 65,536 IPs)
Subnets: public (with IGW route) and private (no direct internet)
Route Tables: control traffic flow between subnets and gateways
NAT Gateway: lets private subnet instances access the internet (outbound only)
Security Groups (stateful) and NACLs (stateless) for traffic filtering
examTips: Security Groups are STATEFUL (return traffic auto-allowed). NACLs are STATELESS (must allow both directions)
Default VPC: has internet gateway, public subnets, default SG. Custom VPC: everything manual
VPC Peering: connect two VPCs privately (no transitive peering!)
VPC Endpoints: Gateway (S3, DynamoDB) and Interface (everything else via PrivateLink)
Flow Logs: capture IP traffic info for monitoring and troubleshooting

quiz.q: What is the key difference between Security Groups and NACLs?
options: SGs are stateful, NACLs are stateless
NACLs are stateful, SGs are stateless
quiz.explanation: Security Groups are stateful (return traffic is automatically allowed). NACLs are stateless (you must explicitly allow both inbound AND outbound rules).

quiz.q: How does a private subnet instance access the internet?
options: NAT Gateway in a public subnet
quiz.explanation: Private subnet instances route outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway (or NAT Instance) placed in a public subnet. This allows outbound-only internet access.

services.name: Route 53
services.description: Highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS) web service. Routes end users to internet applications by translating domain names to IP addresses.
keyPoints: DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, Alias
Routing policies: Simple, Weighted, Latency, Failover, Geolocation, Geoproximity, Multi-value
Health checks: monitor endpoint health for DNS failover
Alias records: point to AWS resources (free, works at zone apex)
Domain registration: buy and manage domains directly
examTips: Alias records vs CNAME: Alias is free, works at zone apex (naked domain), AWS-native
Failover routing: active-passive with health checks for disaster recovery
Weighted routing: send X% to one endpoint, Y% to another (canary deploys)
Route 53 is a GLOBAL service (not regional)
100% availability SLA — the most available AWS service

quiz.q: Which Route 53 record type can point to an AWS resource AND be used at the zone apex?
quiz.explanation: Alias records are Route 53-specific, free of charge, can point to AWS resources (ELB, CloudFront, S3), and work at the zone apex (unlike CNAME).

services.name: CloudFront
services.description: Fast CDN service that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs with low latency and high transfer speeds via a global network of edge locations.
keyPoints: 450+ edge locations worldwide for low-latency delivery
Origins: S3 bucket, ALB, EC2, HTTP server, MediaStore
Origin Access Control (OAC): restrict S3 access to CloudFront only
Cache behaviors: path-based rules for different content types
Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions for edge compute
examTips: OAC (Origin Access Control) replaces OAI for S3 origin restriction
Signed URLs: single file access. Signed Cookies: multiple file access
Cache invalidation: costs money. Use versioned file names instead
CloudFront Functions: lightweight (JS only), viewer request/response. Lambda@Edge: full Lambda, all 4 event types
Field-level encryption: encrypt specific POST fields at edge

quiz.q: What should you use instead of cache invalidation for cost-effective content updates?
quiz.explanation: Using versioned file names (cache busting) is free and instant, while cache invalidation requests cost money and take time to propagate.

services.name: ELB
services.description: Automatically distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets (EC2 instances, containers, IPs, Lambda functions) in one or more Availability Zones.
keyPoints: ALB (Application): HTTP/HTTPS, Layer 7, path/host routing, WebSockets
NLB (Network): TCP/UDP, Layer 4, ultra-low latency, static IPs
GLB (Gateway): Layer 3, third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS)
CLB (Classic): legacy, Layer 4+7 — avoid for new deployments
Health checks: route traffic only to healthy targets
examTips: ALB: HTTP/HTTPS workloads, content-based routing. NLB: extreme performance, static IP needed
ALB supports path-based routing (/api/* → one target group, /web/* → another)
NLB can handle millions of requests/sec with ultra-low latency
Cross-zone load balancing: ALB always on (free), NLB off by default (costs when enabled)
Sticky sessions (session affinity): bind user to specific target using cookies

quiz.q: Which load balancer type should you choose for a microservices architecture needing path-based routing?
quiz.explanation: ALB (Application Load Balancer) operates at Layer 7 and supports path-based and host-based routing, ideal for microservices.

services.name: API Gateway
services.tagline: Create, publish, and manage APIs at scale
services.description: Fully managed service for creating, publishing, maintaining, monitoring, and securing REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs at any scale.
keyPoints: REST API: full-featured, request validation, WAF integration, caching
HTTP API: simpler, cheaper, lower latency (for Lambda/HTTP backends)
WebSocket API: real-time two-way communication
Stages: deploy to dev, staging, prod with stage variables
Throttling: 10,000 requests/sec default, burst of 5,000
examTips: REST API vs HTTP API: REST has more features (caching, WAF, API keys). HTTP API is cheaper and faster
Usage plans + API keys for rate limiting and monetization
Lambda proxy integration: API Gateway passes full request to Lambda
Canary deployments: route percentage of traffic to new stage
API Gateway cache: reduce backend calls (300MB to 237GB, TTL 0-3600s)

quiz.q: Which API Gateway type is the most cost-effective for a simple Lambda backend?
quiz.explanation: HTTP API is up to 70% cheaper than REST API and has lower latency. Use REST API only when you need features like caching, request validation, or WAF.

services.name: Direct Connect
services.description: Establish a dedicated private network connection from your data center to AWS. Provides more consistent network performance than internet-based connections.
keyPoints: Dedicated connection: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, or 100 Gbps port speeds
Hosted connection: 50 Mbps to 10 Gbps via AWS Partner
Private VIF: access VPC resources. Public VIF: access public AWS services
Transit VIF: access VPCs via Transit Gateway
Lead time: weeks to months to establish (not instant!)
examTips: Direct Connect is NOT encrypted by default — add VPN over DX for encryption
Takes weeks/months to set up — not suitable for urgent connectivity
Direct Connect Gateway: connect to VPCs in multiple regions via single DX
Use VPN as backup while waiting for DX setup (or as failover)
LAG: Link Aggregation Group — bundle multiple DX connections

quiz.q: Is AWS Direct Connect encrypted by default?
options: No, add VPN over DX for encryption
quiz.explanation: Direct Connect provides a private connection but is NOT encrypted. For encryption, run a VPN connection over the Direct Connect link.
